clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 10-week history of pain, swelling, and stiffness in her wrists and small joints of both hands, lasting over 60 minutes every morning. She has no significant past medical history. On examination there is symmetrical synovitis of the MCPs and PIPs bilaterally. RF is positive at high titre, ACPA is positive, CRP is elevated. Plain X-rays of the hands are normal. A diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis is made. She is not pregnant and has no contraindications to standard therapy. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological treatment?
options: Prednisolone 30 mg daily tapering over 12 weeks combined with methotrexate 15 mg weekly plus folic acid 5 mg weekly
Methotrexate 15 mg weekly alone without additional folic acid supplementation
Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily as monotherapy
Sulfasalazine monotherapy titrated to 2 g daily
Add a TNF-inhibitor biologic immediately as first-line treatment
explanation: At first diagnosis of RA, the standard approach is to start prednisolone (30 mg daily reducing by 5 mg every 2 weeks over 12 weeks) simultaneously with methotrexate (starting at 15 mg weekly, escalated to 25 mg weekly) along with folic acid 5 mg weekly to reduce methotrexate toxicity. This combination rapidly suppresses inflammation while the DMARD achieves its delayed onset of action. Folic acid is co-prescribed with methotrexate as standard of care to reduce adverse effects such as mucositis and hepatotoxicity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Prednisolone plus methotrexate with folic acid is the standard first-line regimen at diagnosis per the lecture algorithm.
Methotrexate alone without folic acid is incorrect; folic acid supplementation is mandatory to reduce methotrexate toxicity including mucositis and hepatotoxicity.
Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy is insufficient for active seropositive RA with raised CRP; it is used as part of triple therapy after failure of MTX alone.
Sulfasalazine alone is not first-line; it is added as part of triple therapy (MTX + SSZ + HCQ) when methotrexate monotherapy is insufficient.
Biologic therapy (TNF inhibitors) is reserved for patients who fail csDMARD therapy such as triple therapy or MTX plus another DMARD; it is not a first-line agent.
clinicalPearl: At first diagnosis of RA, start prednisolone and methotrexate together — the steroid bridges until MTX takes effect; always co-prescribe folic acid with methotrexate.
keyClue: Newly diagnosed seropositive RA with no contraindications — the lecture algorithm starts MTX plus prednisolone simultaneously.
commonExamTrap: Students may omit folic acid or select biologic therapy as first-line, which is only indicated after failure of conventional DMARD therapy.
missedConcept: Folic acid is always co-prescribed with methotrexate in RA to reduce mucosal and hepatic toxicity; steroids bridge the gap until MTX takes effect.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man with a 20-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of progressive neck pain, occipital headache, and new-onset weakness and tingling in both arms and legs. He reports difficulty walking and notices his hands feel clumsy. He is on methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine. On examination there is upper motor neurone pattern weakness in all four limbs and hyper-reflexia. Cervical spine flexion markedly worsens his neck pain. What is the most urgent next step in management?
options: Urgent MRI or CT of the cervical spine to assess for atlanto-axial subluxation with spinal cord compression, with cervical immobilisation pending results
Commence high-dose oral prednisolone and arrange routine outpatient neurology review in 4 weeks
Order nerve conduction studies and EMG to evaluate peripheral neuropathy
Increase methotrexate dose and add a TNF inhibitor for better RA disease control
Prescribe analgesia and physiotherapy for mechanical neck pain
explanation: This patient has signs of acute spinal cord compression — upper motor neurone signs in all four limbs — in the context of long-standing seropositive RA. RA can cause atlanto-axial subluxation (partial dislocation at C1-C2) leading to spinal cord compression, a rare but potentially devastating complication. Urgent imaging (MRI or lateral cervical X-rays in flexion/extension, or CT) and cervical spine immobilisation are the immediate priorities; neurosurgical referral for decompression is required. Any delay risks irreversible cord injury.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Urgent imaging and cervical immobilisation are essential — this is a neurosurgical emergency due to cervical cord compression from atlanto-axial subluxation in long-standing RA.
High-dose steroids and routine neurology review are dangerously delayed and inappropriate — cord compression from subluxation requires urgent imaging and possible neurosurgery, not steroids alone.

Nerve conduction studies assess peripheral nerves; this clinical picture with upper motor neurone signs indicates central (cord) pathology requiring urgent spinal imaging, not peripheral nerve studies.
Escalating immunosuppression does not address the mechanical cause of cord compression from subluxation; surgery is needed for decompression.
This is not mechanical neck pain — bilateral upper motor neurone signs in all limbs indicate cord compression requiring emergency assessment, not conservative management.
clinicalPearl: Long-standing seropositive RA can cause atlanto-axial subluxation leading to cervical cord compression — any RA patient with new myelopathy signs requires urgent cervical spine imaging and immobilisation.
keyClue: Bilateral upper motor neurone signs in all four limbs in a patient with long-standing seropositive RA — atlanto-axial subluxation with cord compression until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Students may attribute the neurological signs to peripheral neuropathy (a known RA complication) and miss the upper motor neurone pattern that points to cord compression.
missedConcept: Spinal cord compression from atlanto-axial subluxation is a rare but life-altering emergency complication of long-standing RA requiring urgent imaging and neurosurgical referral.
redFlagReason: Acute spinal cord compression from atlanto-axial subluxation in RA carries a risk of permanent quadriplegia; delay in diagnosis and immobilisation can cause irreversible neurological injury.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old woman with a 15-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis is reviewed in clinic. She complains of recurrent infections. Blood tests reveal: haemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, white cell count 2.1 x10⁹/L (neutrophils 0.8 x10⁹/L), platelets 98 x10⁹/L. Examination reveals bilateral symmetrical small joint synovitis and a palpable spleen 4 cm below the costal margin. Rheumatoid factor is strongly positive. Which of the following best describes this clinical syndrome?
options: Felty's syndrome: seropositive RA with splenomegaly, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, and anaemia
Secondary Sjogren's syndrome complicating rheumatoid arthritis
Reactive haematological changes from long-term methotrexate toxicity
Amyloidosis complicating long-standing rheumatoid arthritis presenting with pancytopenia
Adult-onset Still's disease with haematological involvement
explanation: Felty's syndrome is a rare complication of long-standing seropositive RA characterised by the triad of splenomegaly, neutropenia, and RA — remembered by the mnemonic SANTA (Splenomegaly, Anaemia, Neutropenia, Thrombocytopenia, Arthritis). It occurs almost exclusively in patients with strongly positive rheumatoid factor. Neutropenia predisposes to recurrent bacterial infections. Persistent lymphadenopathy in such patients should raise suspicion for lymphoma, which is more common in long-standing RA.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. This is Felty's syndrome — the classic triad of seropositive RA, splenomegaly, and neutropenia, with anaemia and thrombocytopenia also present.
Secondary Sjogren's syndrome causes dry eyes and dry mouth due to lacrimal and salivary gland infiltration; it does not cause splenomegaly or the haematological abnormalities seen here.
Methotrexate toxicity can cause cytopenias but does not explain splenomegaly; the full clinical picture with strongly positive RF points to Felty's syndrome.
Amyloidosis in RA typically presents with nephrotic syndrome due to renal amyloid deposition; it does not cause splenomegaly with neutropenia as the primary features.

Adult-onset Still's disease is characterised by intermittent fever, rash, arthralgia, and markedly elevated ferritin with negative RF and ANA; it does not fit this clinical picture of long-standing seropositive RA.
clinicalPearl: Felty's syndrome = seropositive RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia (SANTA mnemonic); neutropenia drives recurrent infections and persistent lymphadenopathy should prompt lymphoma screening.
keyClue: Long-standing seropositive RA with splenomegaly plus neutropenia plus anaemia plus thrombocytopenia — the classic SANTA mnemonic of Felty's syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Students may attribute the pancytopenia to methotrexate toxicity and miss the splenomegaly as the key discriminator pointing to Felty's syndrome.
missedConcept: Felty's syndrome is a rare but important complication of long-standing seropositive RA; lymphoma risk is increased in these patients and persistent lymphadenopathy warrants biopsy.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman with established rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate 20 mg weekly, sulfasalazine, and hydroxychloroquine (triple therapy) presents with a sudden painful flare of her right knee over the past week. She has no fever and no signs of infection. Joint examination reveals marked effusion of the right knee with warm but non-erythematous skin. Her DAS28 score was 2.4 (remission) at her last clinic visit 3 months ago. Her regular medications are unchanged. What is the most appropriate immediate management for this isolated joint flare?
options: Intra-articular glucocorticoid injection into the right knee
Start high-dose oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 12 weeks
Switch from triple therapy to a biologic TNF inhibitor
Add a JAK 1 and 2 inhibitor to her current regimen
Aspirate the joint and send fluid for microscopy and culture to exclude septic arthritis, then commence IV antibiotics
explanation: For a patient in remission on established DMARD therapy who develops an isolated single-joint flare without systemic features or signs of infection, intra-articular glucocorticoid injection is the appropriate first-line management. This provides rapid targeted relief without systemic immunosuppression. Escalating to biologics or JAK inhibitors would be premature for a single flare in a patient otherwise in remission. A short course of oral glucocorticoids is an alternative for multi-joint flares.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Isolated joint flares in a patient otherwise in remission on csDMARDs are best managed with intra-articular glucocorticoid injection as the targeted, lowest-risk intervention.
High-dose prednisolone over 12 weeks is appropriate at new diagnosis but is disproportionate for a single-joint flare in a patient already in remission on triple therapy.
Switching to a biologic is premature; biologics are indicated for sustained flares or failure of triple DMARD therapy, not a single-joint flare in an otherwise well-controlled patient.
JAK inhibitors are targeted synthetic DMARDs used when csDMARDs and biologics have failed; they are not appropriate for isolated flare management in a patient in remission.

Septic arthritis is an important differential, but this patient has no fever, no erythema, and her skin is only warm — consistent with an inflammatory flare. Joint aspiration is prudent if infection is genuinely suspected, but antibiotics without confirmed infection would be incorrect.
clinicalPearl: Isolated single-joint flares in a patient otherwise in remission on csDMARDs are best managed with intra-articular glucocorticoid injection; escalation to biologics requires a sustained flare pattern.
keyClue: Single swollen joint flare in a patient in remission (DAS28 2.4) on triple therapy with no fever or erythema — targeted intra-articular steroid, not systemic escalation.
commonExamTrap: Students often escalate to biologics or systemic steroids for any flare; the key is that a single joint flare in an otherwise stable patient warrants intra-articular injection first.
missedConcept: Flare management in established RA follows a tiered approach: isolated joint flares get intra-articular steroids; sustained multi-joint flares may warrant systemic DMARD escalation.

subtopic: Urgent management of myositis with ventilatory failure
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with a 2-day history of worsening breathlessness at rest and difficulty swallowing. Her husband reports she has had progressive difficulty climbing stairs and rising from chairs over the past 6 weeks. On examination she is using accessory muscles of respiration, oxygen saturation is 88% on room air, and she has a violaceous discoloration of both eyelids with periorbital oedema. Purple scaly plaques are noted over the extensor surfaces of her fingers. Creatine kinase is 8,400 U/L. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Admit urgently, provide supplemental oxygen and respiratory support, and administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days
Discharge home with oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and urgent outpatient rheumatology referral
Start oral methotrexate 15 mg weekly and arrange outpatient pulmonary function testing
Perform bronchoscopy to exclude pulmonary infection before commencing any immunosuppression
Refer urgently to neurology for nerve conduction studies to exclude Guillain-Barre syndrome
explanation: This patient has dermatomyositis with respiratory and pharyngeal muscle involvement causing acute ventilatory failure — an emergency. The heliotrope rash (violaceous periorbital discoloration) and Gottron's papules (purple scaly plaques over extensor surfaces of finger joints) confirm dermatomyositis. Hypoxia from respiratory muscle weakness requires immediate oxygen and respiratory support, and high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 3 days) is indicated when oral steroids are insufficient for respiratory or pharyngeal weakness. Airway protection and ITU involvement may be required.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Respiratory muscle involvement in dermatomyositis causing acute hypoxia is a medical emergency requiring ITU-level care and IV pulse methylprednisolone.

Discharging a hypoxic patient with oral steroids is unsafe — respiratory muscle failure in myositis can progress to complete ventilatory failure rapidly and requires inpatient management with IV steroids.
Oral methotrexate has a delayed onset of weeks and is insufficient for acute respiratory failure; IV methylprednisolone is required immediately.

While infection should be considered, delaying treatment in an acutely hypoxic patient with a clear clinical diagnosis of dermatomyositis risks death from respiratory failure; steroids should not wait.

The clinical picture — proximal muscle weakness, heliotrope rash, Gottron's papules, and markedly elevated CK — is diagnostic of dermatomyositis; Guillain-Barre is an ascending peripheral neuropathy with a different clinical pattern.
clinicalPearl: Respiratory or pharyngeal muscle involvement in polymyositis or dermatomyositis is a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent admission, respiratory support, and IV pulse methylprednisolone 1 g/day for 3 days.
keyClue: Heliotrope rash plus Gottron's papules plus proximal muscle weakness plus acute hypoxia (SpO2 88%) — dermatomyositis with respiratory failure requiring emergency IV steroids.
commonExamTrap: Students may prescribe oral prednisolone and outpatient management, missing that respiratory muscle involvement in myositis is an emergency requiring IV high-dose steroids and respiratory support.
missedConcept: Respiratory or pharyngeal muscle involvement in myositis can cause acute ventilatory failure or aspiration — this is a medical emergency requiring IV methylprednisolone, not oral steroids alone.
redFlagReason: Acute ventilatory failure (SpO2 88%) from respiratory muscle involvement in dermatomyositis is life-threatening; failure to provide immediate respiratory support and IV pulse steroids can result in death.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old male non-smoker presents with a 2-month history of progressive proximal muscle weakness — difficulty rising from a low chair and climbing stairs — associated with weight loss of 5 kg. He denies skin changes. Examination reveals grade 4/5 weakness of hip flexors and shoulder abductors bilaterally, with preserved distal strength. Serum creatine kinase is 3,800 U/L. Muscle biopsy shows fibre necrosis and inflammatory cell infiltrate. A diagnosis of polymyositis is confirmed. In addition to initiating treatment, what further investigation is mandatory at this stage?
options: Routine screening for underlying malignancy with CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and age-appropriate cancer screens
Electromyography (EMG) to confirm the inflammatory myopathy pattern
Testing for anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies
Echocardiogram to assess for cardiomyopathy
Lateral cervical spine X-rays in flexion and extension
explanation: Both polymyositis and dermatomyositis are significantly associated with underlying malignancy — either previously diagnosed or undisclosed. The lecture explicitly states that screening for underlying malignancy should be undertaken routinely in all patients with inflammatory myositis. In a middle-aged man with weight loss and newly diagnosed polymyositis, CT imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis combined with age-appropriate cancer screening (e.g. PSA, colonoscopy) is mandatory to exclude an occult cancer as a paraneoplastic cause.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Malignancy screening is mandatory in all newly diagnosed polymyositis or dermatomyositis patients as both conditions are notably associated with occult or concurrent malignancy.
EMG is useful for distinguishing inflammatory from non-inflammatory myopathies but muscle biopsy already confirms the diagnosis; EMG does not replace the mandatory malignancy screening.
Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies are characteristically associated with Sjogren's syndrome, not polymyositis; relevant antibodies in myositis include anti-Jo-1 and other anti-synthetase antibodies.
Echocardiogram may be relevant if cardiac involvement is suspected clinically, but it is not the mandatory investigation at this stage — malignancy screening takes priority.
Lateral cervical spine X-rays are relevant for suspected atlanto-axial disease in rheumatoid arthritis, not for polymyositis.
clinicalPearl: All newly diagnosed polymyositis and dermatomyositis patients must have routine malignancy screening — the conditions are notably associated with occult cancer, either preceding or concurrent with diagnosis.
keyClue: Middle-aged patient with confirmed inflammatory myopathy and unintentional weight loss — mandatory malignancy screening regardless of skin findings.
commonExamTrap: Students focus on confirming the diagnosis with EMG and overlook that malignancy screening is the most important mandatory investigation once the diagnosis of inflammatory myositis is established.
missedConcept: Both PM and DM are associated with occult malignancy; routine cancer screening is mandatory for all patients, not just those with dermatomyositis skin changes.

topic: Management of Sjogren's syndrome with interstitial lung disease
clinicalStem: A 47-year-old woman with known primary Sjogren's syndrome (positive anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies) presents with a 4-month history of progressive exertional dyspnoea and dry cough. She has been using artificial tears and saliva substitutes for 2 years with good symptom control. Her sicca symptoms are unchanged. HRCT of the chest shows bilateral ground-glass opacification with a pattern of non-specific interstitial pneumonia. Pulmonary function tests confirm a restrictive defect with reduced DLCO. Which of the following treatments is most appropriate for this complication?
options: Glucocorticoids combined with cyclophosphamide for progressive interstitial lung disease
Increase the dose of hypromellose eye drops and pilocarpine as her sicca symptoms may be contributing
Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily to address the lung disease
Reassure her that ILD in Sjogren's syndrome is always mild and requires no treatment
Start methotrexate for the pulmonary involvement
explanation: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is a recognised extra-glandular complication of primary Sjogren's syndrome. The lecture explicitly states that immunosuppression is essential for progressive ILD in Sjogren's syndrome, with glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide being the indicated treatment. This is in contrast to sicca symptoms, for which immunosuppression does not help — sicca is managed symptomatically with lubricants, artificial saliva, and pilocarpine.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Progressive ILD in Sjogren's syndrome requires systemic immunosuppression with glucocorticoids plus cyclophosphamide — this is the indicated treatment for organ-threatening disease.
Eye drops and pilocarpine address sicca symptoms only; they have no effect on interstitial lung disease and would not treat this life-limiting pulmonary complication.
Hydroxychloroquine addresses skin and musculoskeletal features in Sjogren's and may help fatigue; it is not effective treatment for progressive ILD which requires stronger immunosuppression.
ILD in Sjogren's can be progressive and fatal; reassurance without treatment is inappropriate for a patient with a documented restrictive defect and worsening symptoms.
Methotrexate is used for severe arthritis in Sjogren's syndrome; glucocorticoids plus cyclophosphamide are the specific agents indicated for progressive ILD in this condition.
clinicalPearl: Immunosuppression does not help sicca symptoms in Sjogren's syndrome, but glucocorticoids plus cyclophosphamide are essential for progressive interstitial lung disease — treat the organ-threatening complication aggressively.
keyClue: Known Sjogren's syndrome (anti-Ro/La positive) plus HRCT-confirmed ILD with worsening restrictive spirometry — immunosuppression with glucocorticoids and cyclophosphamide is indicated.
commonExamTrap: Students may try to treat all features of Sjogren's with the same drugs; the key is that sicca is managed symptomatically while organ-threatening complications like ILD require systemic immunosuppression.
missedConcept: In Sjogren's syndrome, immunosuppression does not relieve sicca but is essential for progressive ILD; glucocorticoids plus cyclophosphamide are the standard regimen for this complication.

topic: Lymphoma risk in primary Sjogren's syndrome
clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with a 12-year history of primary Sjogren's syndrome attends her annual review. She has well-controlled dry eyes and dry mouth with artificial tears and pilocarpine. Today she mentions a painless swelling below her right jaw that has been present for 3 months and has not resolved despite a course of antibiotics. She denies fever, night sweats, or weight loss. Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies are positive. On examination there is a firm 3 cm parotid gland mass. What is the most important next step?
options: Biopsy of the parotid mass to exclude malignant lymphoma
Repeat the antibiotics course as salivary gland infection is the most likely cause
Start hydroxychloroquine to treat the parotid swelling as a manifestation of disease activity
Reassure the patient that parotid swelling is a benign feature of Sjogren's syndrome and arrange review in 6 months
Request anti-Ro and anti-La antibody titres — a rise would confirm active disease causing the swelling
explanation: Primary Sjogren's syndrome is associated with a 40-fold increased lifetime risk of lymphoma, though the absolute risk remains low. Non-resolving lymphadenopathy or parotid gland enlargement that persists despite antibiotics must trigger a biopsy to exclude malignant lymphoma — this is explicitly stated in the lecture. A firm, persistent parotid mass in a Sjogren's patient requires urgent tissue diagnosis, not reassurance or repeated antibiotics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Non-resolving salivary gland enlargement in a patient with primary Sjogren's syndrome must be biopsied to exclude lymphoma — this is a 40-fold elevated risk condition.
The mass failed to resolve with antibiotics, making infection unlikely; repeated antibiotics delay the critical diagnosis of possible lymphoma.
Hydroxychloroquine treats skin and musculoskeletal features of Sjogren's; it does not treat or exclude lymphoma, which is the priority in a persistent firm parotid mass.
Reassurance is inappropriate — a persistent, firm parotid mass in a patient with 40-fold elevated lymphoma risk requires urgent tissue biopsy, not watchful waiting for a further 6 months.
Anti-Ro/La titres reflect autoimmune activity but do not distinguish between reactive parotid swelling and lymphomatous transformation; biopsy is the only way to exclude malignancy.
clinicalPearl: Primary Sjogren's syndrome carries a 40-fold increased lifetime risk of lymphoma — any persistent, non-resolving salivary gland enlargement or lymphadenopathy must be biopsied to exclude malignancy.
keyClue: Long-standing primary Sjogren's syndrome with a firm parotid mass persisting 3 months after antibiotics — lymphoma must be excluded by biopsy.
commonExamTrap: Students may attribute parotid swelling to Sjogren's-related sialadenitis and delay biopsy; persistence after antibiotics and firmness demand urgent tissue diagnosis.
missedConcept: A 40-fold increased lifetime lymphoma risk in primary Sjogren's syndrome means any non-resolving lymph node or salivary gland enlargement must be biopsied — never assume benign cause.

clinicalStem: A 43-year-old woman with recently diagnosed primary Sjogren's syndrome presents for management of her main symptoms: persistent dry eyes and severely dry mouth affecting eating and speech. Anti-Ro and anti-La antibodies are positive. Schirmer test shows 3 mm wetting in 5 minutes. Artificial tears during the day and lubricating gel at night provide partial relief. She has tried chewing gum for xerostomia with moderate benefit. She asks about a medication to stimulate her own gland secretions. Which of the following drugs is most appropriate to try at this stage?
options: Pilocarpine 5 mg orally in divided doses daily to amplify residual glandular function
Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg twice daily as the first-line treatment for dry eyes and dry mouth
Prednisolone 30 mg daily to suppress the lymphocytic infiltration of salivary and lacrimal glands
Cyclophosphamide and glucocorticoids to reverse glandular fibrosis
Methotrexate 15 mg weekly to treat the sicca features
explanation: Pilocarpine (5–30 mg daily in divided doses) is a muscarinic agonist that amplifies residual glandular secretory function and is worthwhile in early Sjogren's disease where some glandular function remains. The Schirmer test result of 3 mm (normal >6 mm) confirms lacrimal gland dysfunction. Pilocarpine is explicitly recommended in the lecture for this purpose. It does not modify the underlying disease but can meaningfully improve quality of life by stimulating residual secretory capacity.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Pilocarpine is the appropriate secretagogue for amplifying residual glandular function in early Sjogren's — it is a muscarinic agonist that stimulates salivary and lacrimal secretion.
Hydroxychloroquine addresses skin and musculoskeletal features and may help fatigue in Sjogren's; it is not the primary treatment for sicca symptoms and is not a secretagogue.
Prednisolone at 30 mg daily is appropriate for RA at diagnosis but is not indicated for sicca symptoms in Sjogren's — immunosuppression does not improve dry eyes or dry mouth.
Cyclophosphamide plus glucocorticoids are reserved for organ-threatening complications such as progressive ILD or interstitial nephritis; they do not improve glandular function for sicca symptoms.
Methotrexate is used for severe inflammatory arthritis in Sjogren's; the lecture does not indicate it for sicca features.
clinicalPearl: Pilocarpine (muscarinic agonist) amplifies residual glandular secretion in early Sjogren's syndrome and is the appropriate drug when patients need more than symptomatic lubricants for xerostomia and xerophthalmia.
keyClue: Patient with confirmed Sjogren's syndrome (low Schirmer test, anti-Ro positive) asking for a drug to stimulate her own gland secretions — pilocarpine is the muscarinic agonist for this.
commonExamTrap: Students may select hydroxychloroquine for all Sjogren's features; remember it helps musculoskeletal and fatigue symptoms but is not a secretagogue for sicca.
missedConcept: Pilocarpine is the drug of choice to stimulate residual glandular function in Sjogren's — immunosuppressants do not improve sicca symptoms; management is symptomatic.

subtopic: Exercise and lifestyle in knee osteoarthritis
clinicalStem: A 68-year-old obese woman (BMI 34 kg/m²) presents with bilateral knee pain for 2 years, worse on walking and stair climbing, briefly stiff in the mornings for 10 minutes, and relieved by rest. Plain X-rays show bilateral medial tibio-femoral joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and marginal osteophytes. Blood tests are normal. She is reluctant to take medications. Which single intervention would have the most substantial beneficial effect on her knee pain?
options: Weight loss through diet and exercise, as obesity is a major modifiable risk factor and probably one of the most effective treatments for lower limb OA
Quadriceps strengthening exercises alone without weight loss advice
Referral for immediate total knee replacement given bilateral radiological evidence of OA
Commence long-term oral NSAIDs as first-line pharmacological therapy
Prescribe intra-articular hyaluronic acid injections bilaterally
explanation: The lecture explicitly states that weight loss has a substantial beneficial effect on symptoms in obese patients with lower limb OA and is probably one of the most effective treatments available. Obesity is the strongest modifiable risk factor for progression of knee OA. Non-pharmacological measures including weight loss and aerobic exercise are recommended before or alongside pharmacological therapy. Surgery is not indicated at this stage without failure of optimal medical and lifestyle therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Weight loss is described in the lecture as probably one of the most effective treatments available for OA of the lower limbs in obese patients.

Quadriceps strengthening is beneficial in knee OA and should be advised, but weight loss has the most substantial single effect in an obese patient and addresses the root cause of load-related cartilage damage.

Total knee replacement is indicated when symptoms significantly impair quality of life despite optimal medical therapy and lifestyle advice — not as first-line management; surgery at this stage would be premature.

Oral NSAIDs are more effective than paracetamol and are appropriate for symptomatic patients, but they are not the most impactful single intervention in an obese patient where weight loss addresses the mechanical load directly.
Intra-articular hyaluronic acid is effective in knee OA but expensive and short-lived; weight loss is more impactful long-term and addresses the underlying cause of disease progression.
clinicalPearl: Weight loss in obese patients with lower limb OA is probably the single most effective treatment available — it reduces mechanical load on cartilage and significantly improves symptoms.
keyClue: Obese patient (BMI 34) with bilateral knee OA asking about non-drug management — weight loss is the most effective single intervention according to the lecture.
commonExamTrap: Students may jump to oral NSAIDs or surgery; the question tests knowledge that lifestyle modification (weight loss) is the most impactful intervention for obese patients with lower limb OA.
missedConcept: Obesity is a major modifiable risk factor for OA progression; weight loss produces the most substantial symptomatic benefit in lower limb OA and should be the cornerstone of management in obese patients.

topic: Clinical features distinguishing OA from RA
subtopic: Morning stiffness as a differentiating feature
clinicalStem: A 65-year-old retired farmer presents with knee pain that has been worsening over 3 years. The pain is worst after prolonged activity and initially stiff in the mornings for about 10 minutes before easing. He reports 'good days and bad days'. On examination the right knee has bony swelling around the joint margin, coarse crepitus on movement, and mild periarticular tenderness. There is no warmth or synovial thickening. ESR is 18 mm/h, CRP is normal, RF is negative. Which feature most strongly supports osteoarthritis over rheumatoid arthritis as the diagnosis?
options: Morning stiffness lasting less than 15 minutes that resolves quickly with activity
Bilateral involvement of the knee joints in a symmetrical pattern
The patient's occupation as a farmer involving repetitive loading
Bony swelling and crepitus on examination
Normal ESR and negative RF
explanation: Morning stiffness duration is a key differentiator between OA and RA. In OA, morning stiffness is brief — typically less than 15 minutes — and resolves quickly with movement. In RA, morning stiffness lasts more than 60 minutes and is a defining feature. The term 'gelling' (brief stiffness after rest) in OA contrasts with the prolonged inflammatory stiffness of RA. While all other listed features may support OA, the stiffness duration is the single most discriminating clinical feature between the two conditions.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. Morning stiffness less than 15 minutes is the hallmark of OA; RA produces stiffness exceeding 60 minutes — this duration discriminates the two conditions most reliably.
Bilateral symmetrical involvement occurs in both OA and RA; symmetry alone does not discriminate between them.
Occupational repetitive loading (farming) is a risk factor for OA, providing context, but the clinical feature that most strongly discriminates OA from RA is the stiffness duration.
Bony swelling and crepitus are characteristic OA findings, but these are examination signs of established joint damage — the stiffness duration is the more discriminating early clinical feature.
Normal ESR and negative RF support OA but are not diagnostic alone — seronegative RA exists, and ESR can be normal in early RA; the stiffness duration is more clinically reliable as a discriminator.
clinicalPearl: Morning stiffness in OA lasts less than 15 minutes (brief 'gelling') whereas in RA it lasts more than 60 minutes — this single feature is the most reliable clinical discriminator between the two conditions.
keyClue: Morning stiffness of only 10 minutes in a 65-year-old male farmer with mechanical-pattern knee pain — brief stiffness is the hallmark of OA versus RA's prolonged inflammatory stiffness.
commonExamTrap: Students may focus on bony swelling or negative RF as the discriminating features; the stiffness duration is the single most important clinical differentiator between OA and RA.
missedConcept: OA stiffness is brief (<15 min, 'gelling') and worsened by activity; RA stiffness is prolonged (>60 min) — duration of morning stiffness is the key diagnostic discriminator.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old woman with a 6-year history of bilateral knee osteoarthritis presents for review. She has been on oral NSAIDs, paracetamol, has completed a physiotherapy course with quadriceps strengthening, and has lost 8 kg over the past 18 months. She has received three intra-articular glucocorticoid injections over 12 months. Despite all of this, she is now unable to climb more than 2 stairs unaided, cannot walk more than 100 metres, and is unable to perform activities of daily living independently. Standing X-rays show almost complete loss of medial tibio-femoral joint space bilaterally with subchondral sclerosis and osteophytes. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for orthopaedic assessment for total knee replacement surgery
Add intravenous immunoglobulin as salvage therapy for refractory OA
Prescribe strong opiates as long-term maintenance analgesia
Repeat the course of intra-articular glucocorticoid injections every 3 months indefinitely
Refer for spinal cord stimulation as an alternative to surgery
explanation: Total joint replacement is indicated when symptoms and functional impairment significantly impact quality of life despite optimal medical therapy and lifestyle advice. This patient has exhausted conservative management (NSAIDs, paracetamol, physiotherapy, weight loss, multiple injections) and has significant structural damage on X-ray with severe functional limitation (cannot climb 2 stairs, cannot walk 100 metres). These are exactly the criteria described in the lecture for surgical referral. Over 95% of joint replacements function well for more than a decade.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct. All conservative measures have been optimised and the patient has significant structural damage with severe functional limitation — the criteria for total knee replacement referral are met.
Intravenous immunoglobulin has no evidence base for osteoarthritis management; it is used in inflammatory conditions and myositis.

Strong opiates are occasionally needed but are not appropriate as a long-term maintenance strategy in an otherwise healthy patient who meets criteria for surgery; they have significant side effects without addressing the structural problem.

Intra-articular glucocorticoid injections every 3 months have shown efficacy for up to 1 year, but this patient has already had multiple injections and continues to deteriorate — repeating them indefinitely is not appropriate when surgical criteria are met.
Spinal cord stimulation is not a standard treatment for knee OA; it is used for refractory neuropathic pain conditions.
clinicalPearl: Total knee replacement is indicated when quality of life is significantly impaired despite optimal conservative therapy — cannot walk a block or climb a flight of stairs are common functional thresholds for surgical referral.
keyClue: Bilateral knee OA with complete failure of NSAIDs, physiotherapy, weight loss, and multiple steroid injections, plus inability to walk 100 m or climb stairs — surgical referral criteria are met.
commonExamTrap: Students may offer more conservative options despite the patient having already exhausted them; recognising when the threshold for surgical referral is met is the key clinical skill tested here.
missedConcept: Surgery for OA is indicated when quality of life is significantly impaired despite optimal medical and lifestyle therapy — total joint replacement is the most common procedure and is highly effective.
